speaker: interviewer
text: November the 10, 1995. The survivor is Lola Snow. The interviewer is Vera Goldberg, and the city is Sydney, Australia. And the language will be in English. My name is Vera Goldberg. And I will be doing this interview today with Lola Snow. Can you tell me your name, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Lola Snow.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your name when you were born?

speaker: interviewee
text: Lola Majerczyk.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: On the 24th [INAUDIBLE], 1922.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how old-- how old does that make you now?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very old. Over 73.

speaker: interviewer
text: Lola, where were you born? Where?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Bedzin.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where is Bedzin?

speaker: interviewee
text: Bedzin is in Poland. It's a little city-- not so little, because it was densely populated. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] it had about 30,000 people living there, and 25,000 were Jews.

speaker: interviewer
text: This was when you were a little girl?

speaker: interviewee
text: When I was a little girl.

speaker: interviewer
text: What can you tell me about Bedzin, other than the very big Jewish population?

speaker: interviewee
text: Bedzin was a city where there were-- there-- there were a lot-- a lot of [NON-ENGLISH] in Bedzin. And there were-- in Bedzin, you-- you had-- there was a-- it wasn't a historical city. It was a small city.

speaker: interviewee
text: And what can I tell you about it? There were very, very people who loved to learn, education of people. There were many gymnasiums. I was going to a co-ed gymnasium, which was called [INAUDIBLE] at the beginning.

speaker: interviewee
text: But then, one of the richest people in Bedzin offered a big-- built a big school. And the whole building he dedicated for that school. And it was called a gymnasium by the name of [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: That was after the man?

speaker: interviewee
text: But at the beginning, when my brother was going still to school, it was called [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: And tell me, which part of Poland was Bedzin in?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was called [NON-ENGLISH]. [NON-ENGLISH] is like Dabrowa, Benzin, Sosnowiec. And it was very close to Katowice. And Katowice was already Silesian, you know?

speaker: interviewer
text: So it was in-- in-- Katowice was the closest big city?

speaker: interviewee
text: Big city. But Sosnowiec had 100,000 people. There was no villages. They were cities.

speaker: interviewer
text: A series of-- of cities in that area?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me a little bit about your family.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born when my parents were pretty old. That means they never expected anymore that they'll have a child. I had one brother which was about 13 years older than myself.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: We called him Yumek, but his name was Benjamin. Benjamin Lejbus was his real name, but we all called him Yumek. So my recollection of my brother as a child are very, very dimmed.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because he probably left for university, which he had to go to another city or another country when I was five, you know? So he studied in Warsaw. Then he studied in Vienna.

speaker: interviewee
text: But he was very, very fond of me, you know, like a-- like a kid. And he was proud, because I was very bright. I-- I-- my mother always says that I  stayed with my childhood brains.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you-- you had a really happy relationship with your brother that you--

speaker: interviewee
text: Whatever it was, a relationship, you know. I had a happy relationship. But I can remember that when we went somewhere for holidays, you know, in holiday plays, and he was there already, a young man, a young teenager, and I was that high. And I loved to watch him dancing-- you know, at this time they were dancing in-- in open air. And he-- he wouldn't even recognize me there. That stayed with me. And I said, one day I show you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about your father. What did your father do?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father had a bank.

speaker: interviewer
text: It was his bank?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was his bank, yes. And as it-- my father was quite a well-to-do man. We had-- we had quite a few buildings that belonged to us. And the last building he built together with a partner. And it was finished in 1937. And that was the first intercom that was invented. So it was [NON-ENGLISH], you know, unexpected, something we didn't know what it is.

speaker: interviewer
text: And tell me, what sort of man was your father?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father was a man-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I tell you what, I-- I've written down everything. But I can tell, my father was a man who loved to give-- to be-- to help anybody. He had seven sisters and brothers. He helped every one of them. He married off every one of them with-- with big dowries. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Was he the oldest?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was the oldest.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was he in his family?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was the oldest. He was the oldest. He looked even after his father, you know. And he was really the benefactor of the whole of his family, and also helped on my mother's side, too, when somebody needed. He-- he was a man, you know-- I once belonged-- when I was going to [INAUDIBLE], there was once an essay given to us. And in the essay they asked me, if you have ever met a [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: What is a [NON-ENGLISH]?

speaker: interviewee
text: And what is a [NON-ENGLISH]? And I said, I said that I most probably didn't mean the [NON-ENGLISH] in the full meaning of the word. But I consider my father a [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: So he was a wise man?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was a wise man, and a man who lived to be-- to do good for other people.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was he a religious man?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: How religious?

speaker: interviewee
text: Religious-- pretty religious, but he was lenient. He looked like this when it came, because I was a bit of a rebel. I wanted to go to pictures on Saturday, which wasn't really allowed. So he used to open his safe for me that I take the money myself.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he-- he was an orthodox observer and man.

speaker: interviewee
text: Orthodox observer and man. He was a [NON-ENGLISH]. You know, in Poland, there were people which-- Jews which worshipped this rabbi or this rabbi or this rabbi. He was a worshipper of the [NON-ENGLISH] rabbi.

speaker: interviewer
text: So even though he-- he followed the Hassidic tradition--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --he allowed you a certain amount of freedom?

speaker: interviewee
text: He allowed me a certain-- he looked like that, over the finger, through the finger.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did he wear modern clothing?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. He wore a long coat in-- in our city and a cap. And he-- he had no payos. But he had his head shaved, and he had a beard.

speaker: interviewer
text: So--

speaker: interviewee
text: He was-- he was exceptionally good looking, tall, handsome, with blue eyes. And he adored me.

speaker: interviewer
text: So--

speaker: interviewee
text: And I adored him.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you had a very special relationship with him? Tell me about your mother.

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother wanted the best for me. But it's very hard to be always the best, you know? And she kept me very, very strict. If she came to school and they said that I didn't know something, she sat with me, not knowing even what I was-- she bought a map or she bought this. And I had to know every city and every river on that map, which wasn't even necessary. But she kept me so strict, because she wanted me to be the best. But whatever I am today, if it's anything left of me, I have to thank my mother for it. Because she really wanted me to be educated.

speaker: interviewee
text: My father didn't think it's necessary for a young girl to be educated. Because he said, what for? I-- I shall buy you a [INAUDIBLE]. I shall buy you a doctor. And you don't need to have any education.

speaker: interviewee
text: But my mother insisted, and I went to school. And I was learning piano, and I was learning dancing. I was-- you know, I was really having the best of everything. But God forbid I came a minute late home. My mother was furious. And I was scared of my mother.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the relationship that you had with your parents was different, the relationship you had with your father?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Let's say, every so often there used to be information in school, how you do-- it's how you are doing in school. The mothers used to go. It was always on Sunday. And that Sunday, when I stayed with my father, father home, we both had tummy aches.

speaker: interviewer
text: So your mother was actually the disciplinarian--

speaker: interviewee
text: The disciplinarian.

speaker: interviewer
text: --in the family.

speaker: interviewee
text: But she wanted the best for me. But I was scared.

speaker: interviewer
text: So tell me, was your mother as religious as your father?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I would say she-- she-- she was religious. But I remember that I had a birthday party maybe when I was eight. And my mother was wearing a piece of a wig-- not a whole wig, but a piece. And I said, please, Mom, take it off for my birthday party. She took it off. She never put it on anymore. And my father never knew that she hasn't got it, because she kept doing her hair that way, that it looked like a wig.

speaker: interviewer
text: So she-- she-- she in fact, was not quite as traditionally observant as he was?

speaker: interviewee
text: She was observant. She was kosher. And she [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] worshipping Saturday and all holiday, and obeying everything. But my father used to go three times a day to the [NON-ENGLISH]. In the morning, he went to the [NON-ENGLISH]. For [INAUDIBLE], he went again to the [NON-ENGLISH]. And I remember, I was always so scared if he comes home. OK, because I was scared, because of his beard. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Why? Let's talk about this fear. What were you scared of?

speaker: interviewee
text: The anti-Semitism in Poland. The Poles were just as big anti-Semites, even bigger than the German. If they wouldn't be the collaboration of them, they wouldn't be able to kill so many people.

speaker: interviewer
text: So even as a little girl, you grew up with fear?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, maybe not a little girl. When I was an early teenager, I grew up with fear. When I passed the policeman, I was scared. I-- I was-- I didn't-- I didn't feel guilty of anything.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you experience anti-Semitism yourself? Had-- were you in any situations where you were frightened?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No, I don't remember. I don't remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you knew that it happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: That it happens all the time. And especially, I was afraid-- afraid of my father. Because he had the beard and the dress that he was wearing.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he looked so Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: He looked so Jewish.

speaker: interviewer
text: That made him vulnerable.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was afraid.

speaker: interviewer
text: You mentioned that you were a rebellious girl, a modern girl. Tell me about yourself as a little girl.

speaker: interviewee
text: What I remember-- see, I-- I wasn't allowed many things to do. I wasn't allowed to-- to belong to the Zionistic organization, for instance. Because as my father was a very charitable person, he never gave any money for Palestine.

speaker: interviewer
text: He didn't believe in Zionism?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no. So I wasn't-- I wasn't allowed to belong to a Zionistic organization. And I suffered because of this, that I never went there

speaker: interviewer
text: In what way did you suffer?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because I wanted to be in it, too, with all my friends, you know? And I couldn't. So I-- I-- I was a rebel. I was going out with boys when I was 12. It was not unheard of, you know?

speaker: interviewer
text: Who knew about that? Did your parents know?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this was a big secret?

speaker: interviewee
text: Big secret-- I was going to pictures and kissing in pictures.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where did you meet these-- these--

speaker: interviewee
text: They were school friends. I was going to a co-ed school. And I always had a boyfriend, as ugly as I was.

speaker: interviewer
text: And tell me, at the-- at the co-ed-- the co-ed school was a Jewish school.

speaker: interviewee
text: Jewish school.

speaker: interviewer
text: A Jewish co-ed school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. We had three hours a week of Hebrew, two hours of religion, two hours of Bible, and two hours of Jewish history, in Hebrew.

speaker: interviewer
text: The classes were mixed?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mixed.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were all the classes mixed?

speaker: interviewee
text: All the classes. All the classes were co-ed.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you had ample opportunity to--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --to carry on a flirtation.

speaker: interviewee
text: I always had to have a boyfriend. Nothing happened ever. But you had to think of somebody, you know?

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you ever caught? Did your parents find out?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, my mother most probably knew that I am going with this-- the boys were coming home, too. It wasn't like-- as school friends, they are allowed to come home and sit down and-- and learn with-- learn with me, you know, make homework with me or things like this.

speaker: interviewee
text: But going alone with a boy, that was another thing, you know? I remember it was once-- of-- on top of it, we had a gymnasium where just gentile people were going. And looked at-- we liked the gentile boys, too. And there was a special gentile boy by the name of Staszek Snowsowski. And every girl was in move with him.

speaker: interviewee
text: And that Staszek Snowsowski, after being with this one and this one, suddenly started to flirt with me. And I couldn't understand why. But you know, I was flabbergasted. I was-- I was-- it was-- it was something that I was very happy about. But I was scared. So I remember, one Friday afternoon, we had a rendezvous. And I-- I met him. And then I [INAUDIBLE] collect another girlfriend. And I said, Freida, come with me. Because I don't want by myself to go with Staszek on [INAUDIBLE] where everybody will see me. And I was going with Freida. And suddenly, Freida left me. And I was going by myself with him. I got so scared that I said, look, I have to say goodbye to you. Because I forgot, I have to collect some-- some papers from a girlfriend of mine or some schoolbooks. And I said goodbye to him. And I went to that girlfriend, collected what I-- what I didn't or had and was going up the street. It was Friday afternoon. You have to know, Friday afternoon was half dead in Bedzin, because everybody was preparing for the Shabbos. Suddenly, I see my father going down the street.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I said, what are you doing here? And he said, what are you doing here? And I said, I am just coming home. Somebody rang me up that you are here with a shegetz. A shegetz? Being--

speaker: interviewee
text: A goy. A goy.

speaker: interviewer
text: A non-Jewish boy?

speaker: interviewee
text: A non-Jewish boy. So I came down to see it. So I was so lucky that he didn't caught me.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, what happened? Was that the end of your relationship with this boy?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not really. It wasn't much of a relationship. We were walking the street and looking at each other. It was-- it was 60 years ago or 50-odd years ago.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me a little bit more about the school.

speaker: interviewee
text: That school was-- I have the most beautiful memories from my school. Because that school was a second home to me. In the afternoon, I used to go to ice skating, play tennis, go to the library, meet my friends, play all sorts of games there. We used to meet-- the school was situated very close to where we lived. And I loved that school. And I think my happiest years were at the school.

speaker: interviewer
text: Because of the friendships and the activities?

speaker: interviewee
text: The friendships still today, the connection-- not many of us are left. But the ones that are left, we're still in contact. When I go to Israel, there are reunions all the time of the school friends of mine.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that's something that has endured through everything?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of school was it? Was it an academic type of school?

speaker: interviewee
text: The academic that was the highest standard of academic, so high that when we were done our matura, which is when we were doing matura--

speaker: interviewer
text: Matura is the--

speaker: interviewee
text: The highest school certificate.

speaker: interviewer
text: The graduation.

speaker: interviewee
text: They didn't-- they didn't send anybody from the [INAUDIBLE] to be present. Our director was good enough to be present there.

speaker: interviewer
text: And graduating from the school enabled you to study anything you wished?

speaker: interviewee
text: Anything.

speaker: interviewer
text: And anywhere?

speaker: interviewee
text: Anywhere. Not in Poland, because in Poland, it was [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewee
text: Some [INAUDIBLE] had-- faculties had [NON-ENGLISH]. That means that nobody was accepted.

speaker: interviewer
text: No-- you mean no Jew?

speaker: interviewee
text: No Jew. No Jewish person was accepted. And some had [NON-ENGLISH], which mean that only a closed number-- closed number of people that were-- that were able to enter the university. And they had to sit on the left side, because they were Jews. It was terrible, absolutely terrible. I-- I had never been there, because I was too young. But I heard about it, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your brother went through the system?

speaker: interviewee
text: My brother started-- we-- my brother finished school, most probably, in 1920. Yes, you can see 1923, he's quite a big boy there. And then he studied in Warsaw, and then he studied in Vienna.

speaker: interviewer
text: So did many Jewish students study outside of Poland?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, all his friends-- I used to know all those names, like [INAUDIBLE] and Nancy, all because they were the universities where my-- where the friends of my brother were studying. Nobody studied in Poland. I met later on, a friend, who studied medicine. Also, that was already during the war. He studied in [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: So people had to go elsewhere.

speaker: interviewee
text: Elsewhere.

speaker: interviewer
text: All the time? No matter how good a student they were?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know any-- I-- I have a friend here who studied law in Warsaw University. But he-- she had to sit on the left side.

speaker: interviewer
text: So there was this constant--

speaker: interviewee
text: Constant.

speaker: interviewer
text: --that you were Jewish and you were different.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, we were different.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about in the actual city of Bedzin itself, which had such a large Jewish population?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you feel safe in the city? Or--

speaker: interviewee
text: I felt

speaker: interviewer
text: I mean, you--

speaker: interviewee
text: I felt--

speaker: interviewer
text: You said you were worried about your father.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Because I was hearing all the time about the pogroms here, pogroms there. I never occurred a pogrom in Bedzin, but I was scared. In Bedzin, there were, as I told you, there were a few doctors, non-Jewish. There was most probably some solicitors non-Jewish. And there were caretakers non-Jewish and, you know, servants non-Jewish.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have--

speaker: interviewee
text: And there were maybe a few shops non-Jewish. But on Saturday, you felt it in every corner that it's Shabbos, because everything was closed.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it was essentially a completely Jewish city?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: You mentioned your dad was a banker.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you had a comfortable childhood?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about your house.

speaker: interviewee
text: The last-- we lived at first, when I was a little girl-- we really didn't live so comfort. But I tell you, most probably from 1934 how we lived. We had a huge, what you call here, a unit, that belonged to us. The whole house belongs to us. The magistrate of the city was out tenant. And it was in the same house.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I had a beautiful room, all in pink. And it must have been big, because there was a piano in that room. It was a beautiful couch in that room, which was like my bed there. There was a round table, with chairs. I had a desk which, when I turned it, it became, like, like, my makeup cabinet. And it was a big, big wardrobe and-- and a big oven. You had to have a [INAUDIBLE] oven, you know? Ah, for heating.

speaker: interviewee
text: For heating.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you-- you had servants at home?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were your servants Jewish or not Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: One was Jewish, one was not Jewish. One who was cooking was Jewish and one who was-- but not always. We had two. Sometimes we had only one non-Jewish. And my mother was cooking. And she was helping. But mostly, we had two.

speaker: interviewer
text: Other than the-- the servants that you had at home and the--

speaker: interviewee
text: And I was always friends with every servant.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah, I was going to ask you.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have non-Jewish friends? Other than-- you mentioned the boy, the non-Jewish boy.

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No, not really, friends. I knew them, but I couldn't call them friends.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you wouldn't go to their houses, or they wouldn't come to your house or anything like that?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, I don't remember that. No. But I-- every servant was always my best friend. And my mother always was angry that I-- that-- that's not your place to be in the kitchen and to talk to the servant.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you enjoyed that?

speaker: interviewee
text: But I-- I-- loved it.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were your leisure activities beside-- you mentioned the movies to me. And what other things did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: You know what? To go for a walk was a big leisure at that time. If we had time after school to go for a walk on the-- on the main street and see everybody who had a new hat and who had new shoes and things like this, that was a big pleasure for us. Then swimming, I loved always swimming. Even we didn't have a-- I-- we only had a river. To go to a swimming pool, I had to go to another city. But I did go. And I used to swim well.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about in winter? What were your wintertime activities?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I was-- I was-- I was going-- I-- I was [INAUDIBLE] skating. And my father even came to look at me when I was skating. And I used to go to Zakopane and to Krynica to be able to ski.

speaker: interviewer
text: How far were Zakopane and Krynica.

speaker: interviewee
text: About 12 hours by train.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you would go for a skiing holiday?

speaker: interviewee
text: A skiing holiday. You wouldn't go there for a day, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long would you stay there?

speaker: interviewee
text: It depends, a fortnight, at least a fortnight.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who would you go with?

speaker: interviewee
text: I used to go with the school.

speaker: interviewer
text: The school organized these trips?

speaker: interviewee
text: School organized skiing. And I always used to go with my mother.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did she ski?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. But she always used to like to go everywhere with me. When was excursion, let's say, to [INAUDIBLE], which was the-- the poor city in-- in Poland, she came with me. If we went to Krakow, she came with me. She-- if we went skiing, she came with me.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you would go on these trips on a regular basis? You'd go quite often?

speaker: interviewee
text: Quite often. Quite often.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have holidays with your parents? Did your parents take holidays?

speaker: interviewee
text: I tell you, my parents have holidays. And I used to go on holidays with my mother, always, till I was about 13 or 14. Then they used to send me with the school. And they went on a-- you know, to Czechoslovakia or somewhere like this on holidays. And then-- then when my father died, I, again, was always with my mother on every holiday. But that's wasn't very long anymore.

speaker: interviewer
text: Sure. And in the meantime, while you were growing up, your brother had left home already? He had--

speaker: interviewee
text: My brother got married in 1934. So he left home. And-- but he lived with his wife in our city. And he was very, very fond of me. And he used to come-- I remember when there was dance music on the radio, he used to come and to try to dance with me.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when you-- when you look back on--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --your childhood and your early teenage years, what's the overall impression you have of that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had quite a happy childhood. First of all, because I could afford-- my parents could afford medicine. And then I was given as much attention as possible. And, you know, I was only kept very strict. And that's why I promised myself that I'd give as much freedom as possible to my children.

speaker: interviewer
text: Because you really didn't have it? OK. We'll stop there.
